The vision shifts now to practical detail—chambers for priests, measurements, boundaries. After chapters of fire and judgment and the return of God's glory, Ezekiel is shown where His people will actually work and worship. This is not abstract theology. It is a temple that will function, with rooms for storing offerings, hallways for movement, doors for access. The divine presence requires structure. Holiness demands order.

Notice what the guiding figure tells Ezekiel about these chambers: the priests who approach the Lord will eat the most holy offerings here. This is intimate proximity to God, purchased at great cost. But that proximity carries a weight the priests cannot simply carry outside the temple walls. They must change their garments before returning to the places where common people dwell. The clothes worn in God's presence become holy themselves—so holy that they cannot cross into ordinary space without defiling it. This is not cruelty or arbitrary restriction. It reflects something true: you cannot stand in God's presence and remain unchanged. The holiness clings to you. It marks you. And that marking creates a necessary separation, a boundary that protects both the holy and the common from being confused or corrupted by proximity.

The final measurement is almost startling in its simplicity. A wall all around—five hundred cubits on every side, creating a perfect square. The guide has finished his work. The interior is mapped. The purpose of each space is declared. And now the boundary itself is measured: it exists to separate the holy from the common. This is the fundamental function of the entire structure. Not to showcase God's power through artistic beauty—though the building is surely magnificent—but to maintain a distinction that cannot be erased. In a world where everything tends toward sameness and compromise, where the sacred and secular blur into each other, this temple stands with clear edges.

For those who know Christ, this vision points toward something deeper still. He is the true Temple, the place where God's presence dwells fully and finally. He is also the great High Priest who enters the holy place, who bears the mark of His ministry—the wounds of His sacrifice—forever. And He has torn the veil between holy and common, making a way for sinners to approach God without mediation. Yet the reality of holiness has not diminished. It has intensified. God's separateness has become not a wall that keeps us out, but a fire that burns away everything in us that cannot stand before Him. The boundary remains. But Christ Himself has become the gate through which we pass.
